Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. -[removes]-> modified layer.| title/DOI:  Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: c8e7b3d3edfa51e3d941c259e848c0a2\nwet cleaning -[alternative method for]-> etching rate.| title/DOI:  Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. -[removes]-> modified layer.| title/DOI:  Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: c8e7b3d3edfa51e3d941c259e848c0a2\nwet cleaning -[alternative method for]-> etching rate.| title/DOI:  Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching.
