Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. -[removes]-> modified layer.| title/DOI:  Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: c8e7b3d3edfa51e3d941c259e848c0a2\nwet cleaning -[alternative method for]-> etching rate.| title/DOI:  Characterization of an Etch Profile at a Wafer Edge in Capacitively Coupled Plasma | chunk_id: 94f7272a2908ed092912e9058265356e\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: SF6 , C4F8 , and O2 were separately used as discharge gases for experiments concerning the above four combinations. The flow rate and the pressure of discharge gases were fixed at 4 sccm and 5 mTorr, respectively. The cathode temperature was maintained at 15 °C during the experiments.\n\nTwo sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the dependences of the bottom and sidewall etch rates on bias voltage and source power. The bias voltage was varied between 0 and 400 V at intervals of 50 or 100 V, while the source power was maintained at 400 W. Here, the bias voltage is represented as having a positive sign, similar to the case of conventional data processing, although the bias voltage is, in fact, negative. In another set of experiments, the source power was varied between 0 and 600 or 800 W at intervals of 200 W, while the bias voltage was maintained at 200 V. For all data presented in this work, the source power is the forward power to the matching network. In general, the ion density is linearly dependent on the source power in the conventional etcher using a rf induction plasma source.13 In addition, we confirmed by Ar actinometry although the results are not shown here, that concentrations of F or O radicals are linearly dependent on the source power.\n\nAccordingly, the ratio of the ion density to radical concentration can be regarded to be nearly independent of the source power. The etching experiments were carried out for 3 min. A fluorocarbon polymer film was etched in an Ar plasma to observe the physical etch characteristics of the bottom and sidewall under the following conditions: 4 sccm, 5 mTorr, 15 °C, 200 W, 0–400 V, and 3 min. | title/DOI: Dependences of bottom and sidewall etch rates on biasvoltage and source power during the etching of poly-Si andfluorocarbon polymer usingSF6,\u2008C4F8,andO2plasmas | chunk_id: 90c9ecf1ecd582ebf080d4e21f441a0a\n\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 135 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. Q:
What are the knobs that can change the uniformity in radical si-etching process?

A:
ChatResult(chat_id=None, chat_history=[{'content': 'What are the knobs that can change the uniformity in radical si-etching process?\n', 'role': 'assistant', 'name': 'user_proxy'}, {'content': 'ANALYSIS:\n- requested answer type: technical explanation\n- overall main gaps: understanding of the radical Si-etching process and its controlling factors\n\nINITIAL QUESTIONS:\n1. QUESTION: What are the key components involved in the radical Si-etching process?\n2. QUESTION: How does the concentration of etching radicals affect the uniformity of the etching process?\n3. QUESTION: What role do temperature and pressure play in controlling the uniformity of the radical Si-etching process?\n4. II. Pattern transfer. | chunk_id: f5162ec6bc91d1e0b1bd0bb18cd9343d\nsilicon -[used in]-> ALE.| title/DOI:  Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: c85b079b2f9bf5d3440e0886b97a6669\nsilicon -[etched selectively to]-> silicon.| title/DOI:  Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: 09958e1815880e2026a046b069dbc10a\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: ## 2.9 Classification Of Etching Technologies\n\nThe naming and classification of an increasing number of etching technologies that are being proposed and already used for patterning of semiconductor devices is sometimes confusing. This situation is becoming exaggerated by the emergence of various ALE implementations. A logical approach to classify all etching technologies is therefore needed.\n\nAll etching technologies for semiconductor devices, with the exception of nonreactive IBE, have a chemical component to weaken the top surface and require energy to remove surface atoms. Based on these common characteristics, it makes sense to use three criteria for classification: (i) the method of surface modification to reduce the surface binding energy from EO to ES (see Figure 2.1),\n(ii) the method or kind of energy for removal, and (iii) whether modification and removal processes proceed simultaneously or in spatially or temporally separated, self-limited, sequential steps. The third criterion delineates ALE methods from continuous etching technologies.\n\nTable 2.3 lists the most relevant continuous etching technologies. RIE is by far the most widely used and important etching method. RIE utilizes reactive plasmas that are in contact with the wafer. The wafer surface is exposed to simultaneous fluxes of reactive and nonreactive ions, neutrals, radicals, electrons, and photons. The surface is modified by reactive ion implantation, as well as adsorption and diffusion of radicals and neutrals. Simultaneously, removal is accomplished by sputtering and desorption. Desorption can be purely thermal, but it can also be enhanced by photons and electrons that are generated by the plasma. To create RIE processes that meet all requirements is a highly complex, and still mostly empirical e\n...[truncated 115 chars] | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: 079fd23dd123d216acf1a492b648f2b8\n\nSource chunk text: Radicals also provide higher etching rates because the adsorption step has no activation barrier. Humbird and Graves performed molecular dynamics simulations of reactions between F*, F2, Cl*, and Cl2 and silicon and found that radicals etch silicon at surface temperatures above 200 K while the molecular species do not etch\n(Humbird and Graves 2004).\n\nThe most common radicals that are used for radical etching are atomic radicals such as F*, Cl*, Br*, O*, and H* as well as smaller molecular radicals such as NO*,\nOH*, NH2\n*, CH*, CH2\n*, CH3\n*, and others. The reason why these radicals contain only a few atoms is the use of high-density plasmas to generate them. High-density plasmas dissociate molecules very efficiently. There are attempts to generate more complex molecules by activation with light, but these are far from implementation.\n\n![96_image_0.png](96_image_0.png)\n\nFigure 5.1 Lennard-Jones diagram for radical etching.\n\n![96_image_1.png](96_image_1.png)\n\n![96_image_2.png](96_image_2.png)\n\n## 5.2 Performance Metrics 5.2.1 Etching Rate And Ernu | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: 3cbabf5d4ffd1099339fc1969cfee25e\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 124 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. II. Pattern transfer. | chunk_id: f5162ec6bc91d1e0b1bd0bb18cd9343d\nsilicon -[used in]-> ALE.| title/DOI:  Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: c85b079b2f9bf5d3440e0886b97a6669\nsilicon -[etched selectively to]-> silicon.| title/DOI:  Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: 09958e1815880e2026a046b069dbc10a\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: ## 2.9 Classification Of Etching Technologies\n\nThe naming and classification of an increasing number of etching technologies that are being proposed and already used for patterning of semiconductor devices is sometimes confusing. This situation is becoming exaggerated by the emergence of various ALE implementations. A logical approach to classify all etching technologies is therefore needed.\n\nAll etching technologies for semiconductor devices, with the exception of nonreactive IBE, have a chemical component to weaken the top surface and require energy to remove surface atoms. Based on these common characteristics, it makes sense to use three criteria for classification: (i) the method of surface modification to reduce the surface binding energy from EO to ES (see Figure 2.1),\n(ii) the method or kind of energy for removal, and (iii) whether modification and removal processes proceed simultaneously or in spatially or temporally separated, self-limited, sequential steps. The third criterion delineates ALE methods from continuous etching technologies.\n\nTable 2.3 lists the most relevant continuous etching technologies. RIE is by far the most widely used and important etching method. RIE utilizes reactive plasmas that are in contact with the wafer. The wafer surface is exposed to simultaneous fluxes of reactive and nonreactive ions, neutrals, radicals, electrons, and photons. The surface is modified by reactive ion implantation, as well as adsorption and diffusion of radicals and neutrals. Simultaneously, removal is accomplished by sputtering and desorption. Desorption can be purely thermal, but it can also be enhanced by photons and electrons that are generated by the plasma. To create RIE processes that meet all requirements is a highly complex, and still mostly empirical e\n...[truncated 115 chars] | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: 079fd23dd123d216acf1a492b648f2b8\n\nSource chunk text: Radicals also provide higher etching rates because the adsorption step has no activation barrier. Humbird and Graves performed molecular dynamics simulations of reactions between F*, F2, Cl*, and Cl2 and silicon and found that radicals etch silicon at surface temperatures above 200 K while the molecular species do not etch\n(Humbird and Graves 2004).\n\nThe most common radicals that are used for radical etching are atomic radicals such as F*, Cl*, Br*, O*, and H* as well as smaller molecular radicals such as NO*,\nOH*, NH2\n*, CH*, CH2\n*, CH3\n*, and others. The reason why these radicals contain only a few atoms is the use of high-density plasmas to generate them. High-density plasmas dissociate molecules very efficiently. There are attempts to generate more complex molecules by activation with light, but these are far from implementation.\n\n![96_image_0.png](96_image_0.png)\n\nFigure 5.1 Lennard-Jones diagram for radical etching.\n\n![96_image_1.png](96_image_1.png)\n\n![96_image_2.png](96_image_2.png)\n\n## 5.2 Performance Metrics 5.2.1 Etching Rate And Ernu | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: 3cbabf5d4ffd1099339fc1969cfee25e\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 124 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. ![33_image_1.png](33_image_1.png)\n\n4.7 nm ALD-grown Al2O3 and 20 nm of ALD tungsten. Reprinted with permission from Lee et al., in International Electron Devices Meeting, 1–5 December 2018\n(IEDM, San Francisco), p. 895. Copyright 2018, IEEE (Ref. 318).\n\nJ. Vac. Sci. Technol. B 42(4) Jul/Aug 2024; doi: 10.1116/6.0003579 42, 041501-33\nthroughput. Low-gap chambers, however, are a challenge for inductively coupled plasmas as the proximity of the inductor to the substrate may result in electrical device damage.\n\n2. Low-energy ions required in directional ALE applications may fail to travel deep into high-aspect-ratio structures due to scattering or recombination. High ion energy ALE may be a solution.292,293 3. Even if ALE removal rates of one monolayer per cycle were assumed, wafer throughput is significantly less than with RIE processes. Etch rates per cycle and thereby wafer throughput can be boosted by high dosing during each of the process steps, by increasing the substrate temperature if the device tolerates it, and by the use of plasma to increase the reactivity.\n\n4. The need to boost wafer throughput brings about a trade-off with the inherently self-limiting nature of ALE reactions, as there may not be ample time for a surface to achieve chemical saturation. Consequently, nonsaturation can lead to undesirable effects such as nonuniform etching from the center to the edge of the wafer, issues with device uniformity from top to bottom, and loading effects related to feature size.\n\n5. In the absence of plasma in both ALE steps, the process must be run at elevated temperatures, in some cases well above 200 °C. The high thermal strain may be an issue for some devices as it accelerates diffusion of atoms across material interfaces.\n\nAdditionally, high temperatures may intr\n...[truncated 74 chars] | title/DOI: Future_of_plasma_etching_for_microelectronics_Challenges | chunk_id: c99fee206df63cbe5ea31832b07b5c26\n\nSource chunk text: Copyright 2022, American Vacuum Society (Ref. 181).\n\nROADMAP ARTICLE pubs.aip.org/avs/jvb\nsequential exposure of a substrate to alternating precursors, resulting in the controlled growth of atomic layers.276 ALE, on the other\n\n![31_image_1.png](31_image_1.png)\n\nhand, aims to selectively remove atomic layers with atomic-scale precision. Similarities and differences between both ALD and ALE\nwere illuminated by Faraz et al.277 Yoder\'s U.S. patent 4756794 published in 1988 is considered the earliest report on this etching technology.278 Figure 30 shows a schematic of the generic ALE\nconcept. Modification and removal steps are self-limited. Surface modification methods include adsorption, diffusion, implantation, conversion, deposition of a reactive layer, extraction, and potentially other methods. The successive removal of the modified surface layer can be activated with either accelerated ions and neutrals, electrons, photons, thermal energy, or chemical reactions.\n\nThese methods may be combined and may be performed thermally or plasma-based leading to a potentially large number of different ALE techniques.\n\nPlasma-enhanced ALE has been studied for over 30 years.31,172 About a decade ago, it became evident that commercial plasma etch reactors could be adapted to perform plasma-enhanced ALE.280,281 This triggered a resurgence of research on this class of ALE. Separation of the etching process into self-limited surface modification and removal steps enables enhanced etching uniformity across the entire wafer and a decrease in ARDE.31 ALE processes produce, in most cases, a smooth etch front.143,282 From a scholarly perspective, plasma-assisted ALE enhances our comprehension of RIE, its continuous etching analog.\n\nIn 2015, researchers at Intel formulated the so-called "fourco\n...[truncated 105 chars] | title/DOI: Future_of_plasma_etching_for_microelectronics_Challenges | chunk_id: a55a9650c2e948636ef81ba7ec8f8bd9\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 215 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 16 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 1 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. ![33_image_1.png](33_image_1.png)\n\n4.7 nm ALD-grown Al2O3 and 20 nm of ALD tungsten. Reprinted with permission from Lee et al., in International Electron Devices Meeting, 1–5 December 2018\n(IEDM, San Francisco), p. 895. Copyright 2018, IEEE (Ref. 318).\n\nJ. Vac. Sci. Technol. B 42(4) Jul/Aug 2024; doi: 10.1116/6.0003579 42, 041501-33\nthroughput. Low-gap chambers, however, are a challenge for inductively coupled plasmas as the proximity of the inductor to the substrate may result in electrical device damage.\n\n2. Low-energy ions required in directional ALE applications may fail to travel deep into high-aspect-ratio structures due to scattering or recombination. High ion energy ALE may be a solution.292,293 3. Even if ALE removal rates of one monolayer per cycle were assumed, wafer throughput is significantly less than with RIE processes. Etch rates per cycle and thereby wafer throughput can be boosted by high dosing during each of the process steps, by increasing the substrate temperature if the device tolerates it, and by the use of plasma to increase the reactivity.\n\n4. The need to boost wafer throughput brings about a trade-off with the inherently self-limiting nature of ALE reactions, as there may not be ample time for a surface to achieve chemical saturation. Consequently, nonsaturation can lead to undesirable effects such as nonuniform etching from the center to the edge of the wafer, issues with device uniformity from top to bottom, and loading effects related to feature size.\n\n5. In the absence of plasma in both ALE steps, the process must be run at elevated temperatures, in some cases well above 200 °C. The high thermal strain may be an issue for some devices as it accelerates diffusion of atoms across material interfaces.\n\nAdditionally, high temperatures may intr\n...[truncated 74 chars] | title/DOI: Future_of_plasma_etching_for_microelectronics_Challenges | chunk_id: c99fee206df63cbe5ea31832b07b5c26\n\nSource chunk text: Copyright 2022, American Vacuum Society (Ref. 181).\n\nROADMAP ARTICLE pubs.aip.org/avs/jvb\nsequential exposure of a substrate to alternating precursors, resulting in the controlled growth of atomic layers.276 ALE, on the other\n\n![31_image_1.png](31_image_1.png)\n\nhand, aims to selectively remove atomic layers with atomic-scale precision. Similarities and differences between both ALD and ALE\nwere illuminated by Faraz et al.277 Yoder\'s U.S. patent 4756794 published in 1988 is considered the earliest report on this etching technology.278 Figure 30 shows a schematic of the generic ALE\nconcept. Modification and removal steps are self-limited. Surface modification methods include adsorption, diffusion, implantation, conversion, deposition of a reactive layer, extraction, and potentially other methods. The successive removal of the modified surface layer can be activated with either accelerated ions and neutrals, electrons, photons, thermal energy, or chemical reactions.\n\nThese methods may be combined and may be performed thermally or plasma-based leading to a potentially large number of different ALE techniques.\n\nPlasma-enhanced ALE has been studied for over 30 years.31,172 About a decade ago, it became evident that commercial plasma etch reactors could be adapted to perform plasma-enhanced ALE.280,281 This triggered a resurgence of research on this class of ALE. Separation of the etching process into self-limited surface modification and removal steps enables enhanced etching uniformity across the entire wafer and a decrease in ARDE.31 ALE processes produce, in most cases, a smooth etch front.143,282 From a scholarly perspective, plasma-assisted ALE enhances our comprehension of RIE, its continuous etching analog.\n\nIn 2015, researchers at Intel formulated the so-called "fourco\n...[truncated 105 chars] | title/DOI: Future_of_plasma_etching_for_microelectronics_Challenges | chunk_id: a55a9650c2e948636ef81ba7ec8f8bd9\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 215 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 16 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 1 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. The radicals are generated in a plasma and then react with the silicon to form a volatile compound that can be removed from the surface.\n   - what should be concerned: The uniformity of the etching process is critical, as non-uniform etching can lead to defects in the final product. Factors that can affect the uniformity of the etching process include the concentration of the radicals, the temperature and pressure of the plasma, and the properties of the silicon wafer.\n**Supporting material 1**\n   - what it is: The concentration of the radicals is a critical factor in determining the uniformity of the etching process. A higher concentration of radicals can lead to a more uniform etch, but it can also increase the risk of defects.\n   - why it matters: The concentration of the radicals affects the rate at which the silicon is etched, and a non-uniform concentration can lead to variations in the etch rate across the wafer.\n   - supporting mechanism or evidence: Studies have shown that the concentration of radicals can be controlled by adjusting the power and frequency of the plasma, as well as the flow rate of the radical-generating gas.\n**Supporting material 2**\n   - what it is: The temperature and pressure of the plasma are also important factors in determining the uniformity of the etching process. Future_of_plasma_etching_for_microelectronics_Challenges | chunk_id: c18e71dc3a4dcca0131bef4f00f69c7e\nRIE -[related to]-> plasmas.| title/DOI:  Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: 4919def4a82a2bc155e6ea4f6ae927a6\n\nRaw source chunk text preview only:\nSource chunk text: Originally, integrated circuits were etched with wet chemical methods using photoresists as a mask. While these methods can be directional for some single crystal materials and selected etchants, removal of amorphous materials etch proceeds in all directions roughly with the same rate. This kind of etching is also called isotropic.\n\nIt works only for features where the lateral dimension is much larger than the thickness of the material to be etched. This property is obviously an obstacle for device shrinking. Another drawback of wet etching is the creation of large amounts of toxic waste.\n\nTo overcome these challenges, dry plasma etching methods were introduced into the manufacturing of integrated semiconductor devices in the 1980s. When a plasma is in contact with a solid surface a phenomenon called sputtering occurs, which causes material removal. Sputtering was discovered by W.R. Grove in 1852. Physical sputtering with noble gas plasmas was used in the 1960s in the electronics industry. When the wafer is placed on a radio frequency (RF) powered electrode, ions are accelerated, and the sputter rate can be increased to make the method more productive (Coburn and Kay 1972). However, physical sputtering is still too slow to be useful in the manufacturing of semiconductor devices. It also critically lacks the selectivity to mask and stop materials. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: f15dd0972c8616029e0bb46ed674bae6\n\nSource chunk text: 1993). The etching rate repeatability was greatly improved at higher temperatures and lower pressures in the absence of a physisorbed layer at the surface. Under the same conditions, heavily boron- and phosphorus-doped SiO2, the so-called boron phosphorus silicon glass (BPSG), etched with a rate of 6000 Å/min. This means that etching selectivities of several hundred to one for different types of SiO2 can be achieved. Thermal etching processes can be extremely selective.\n\nAnother important dry etching approach for SiO2 involves the formation of ammonium hexafluorosilicate, (NH4)2SiF6. This compound sublimates in vacuum at temperatures above 100 ∘C. It can be formed when a mixture of NH3 and NF3 is heated in an applicator tube to temperatures above 600 ∘C (Ogawa et al.\n\n2002). At these temperatures, NF3 decomposes and forms free fluorine, which combines with NH3 to form NH4F in the gas phase. This gas etches SiO2. There is no plasma involved in this process; it is a thermal process where the reactive species are formed by heating of the feed gas mixture. The process can be operated in continuous mode above 100 ∘C wafer temperature. It can also be operated sequentially, where (NH4)2SiF6 salt is formed during the reaction of NH4F with the silicon surface at a temperature below 100 ∘C and removed in a second step where the wafer is rapidly heated above the sublimation temperature. In this mode of operation, this process can be considered a thermal ALE process, albeit an imperfect one, because the growth rate of the salt follows a parabolic law (Ogawa et al. 2002) and therefore is not perfectly self-limited. The underlaying surface reaction can also be used in radical etching processes as will be shown in Chapter 5. | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: 6757b62fc5c18e161fd070ceec1c4231\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 59 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 253 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 147 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching. meaningful degree of saturation can be achieved if the sputtering yield for the modified material is significantly larger than that of the bulk material. EPC slows down visibly even for ksp, 2 = 0.1, which corresponds to a 10-fold increase of the sputtering yield due to surface modification. For smaller ksp, 2, the ALE process becomes closer to ideal and uniformity and ARDE performance are improved.\n\n![111_image_0.png](111_image_0.png)\n\nThis improvement in etching performance for smaller ksp, 2 is the result of increased synergy. The latter is the third critical characteristic of ALE processes besides the existence of an ALE window and saturated or self-limited process steps.\n\nThe definition of synergy is given in Eq. (4.1). For directional ALE,  represents bulk sputtering for processes with ksp, 2 >0. Synergy can also be reduced if material is spontaneously removed during the surface modification step, the amount of which is represented in Eq. (4.1) by the term ∝.\n\nIn the case of ALE with directional removal step such as silicon ALE with surface modification by Cl2 molecules or Cl* radicals and argon ion removal, ∝ can be the result of thermal or radical etching and can be suppressed by choosing appropriate modification gases and operating at lower temperatures.\n\nSystematic studies of directional ALE of various materials revealed that synergy scales with the binding energy of the bulk material, EO (Kanarik et al. 2017). This makes sense because EO determines the sputtering yield Γ and threshold Eth for the bulk material (see Eqs. (2.7) and (2.8)). Figure 6.10a depicts experimental results for the upper limit of the ideal ALE window for various directional ALE processes as a function of the binding energy of the bulk material, EO. Argon ions were\n...[truncated 76 chars] | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: 55588523db75a16865d9aa26c22d3604\n\nSource chunk text: overarching characteristic of all of these ALE processes is that modification and removal proceed in spatially or temporally separated, self-limited, sequential steps.\n\nIt is insightful to subdivide desorption removal into "Desorption 1 (Direct)" and\n"Desorption 2 (Reaction-Assisted)" for ALE processes. While these two types of desorption most likely also exist for continuous etching methods such as RIE, they are not implemented deliberately and hence have been grouped together for simplicity in Table 2.3.\n\nThe fundamentals of "direct" desorption are described in Section 2.3. For direct desorption of a modified layer in an ALE regime, the temperature of the wafer must be cycled. The modification step must proceed at a lower temperature than the removal step or else surface modification and removal proceed simultaneously, and the result is one of the continuous etching technologies in Table 2.3, for instance, thermal or radical etching. The challenge for ALE with temperature cycling is that the cycling between steps must be fast. Because the EPC is typically only a few nanometers, a full cycle should not take more than a few seconds. Depending on the temperature range to be covered in each temperature cycle, the engineering challenges can be daunting.\n\nAs an alternative to thermal ALE with temperature cycling, desorption can also be induced by a second surface reaction (see Section 2.4). In this approach, new adsorbates form at the surface, which can desorb at the same temperature as the surface modification step. This desorption mechanism is labeled "Reaction-Assisted" in Table 2.3. Reaction-assisted desorption is the underlying mechanism for several thermal isotropic ALE reactions such as Chelation/Condensation ALE (see Section 4.1.1), Ligand Exchange ALE (see Section\n...[truncated 95 chars] | title/DOI: Atomic Layer Processing_semiconductor | chunk_id: 757bf1adebe94a58dec8ca9358730ac8\n\nReturn-size / runtime limits applied:\n- 117 mind-map path lines omitted from the tool return by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_MIND_PATHS.\n- 16 raw graph path lines omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_PATHS.\n- 22 raw source chunk texts omitted from preview by QA_RAG_RETURN_MAX_RAW_CHUNKS.\n- 1 source chunks were represented as metadata-only blocks to avoid slow Chroma fetching.
